segments.paradiso-15-g20: Sì is an adverb 'so/thus' modifying the whole clause; pïa is the adjective 'pious' (poetic spelling with diaeresis marking syllabification) describing l’ombra. L’ombra d’Anchise is a noun phrase 'the shade of Anchises'. Si porse is porse (passato remoto of porgere, 'to stretch/hold out') with the reflexive/clitic si, so 'porse' = 'stretched forth / presented itself' in 3rd person singular.
segments.paradiso-15-g21: Se introduces a conditional parenthesis 'if'. Fede is fronted as the direct object 'faith/trust' for emphasis. Merta functions as an archaic verb form of 'meritare' meaning 'deserves' (3rd person singular), so the clause reads 'if our greater Muse deserves faith'. Nostra maggior musa is the subject phrase 'our greater Muse' placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.paradiso-15-g22: Quando = 'when' (temporal). In Eliso 'in Elysium' gives the place. Del figlio is the object of the verb (literally 'of the son'). S’accorse is the passato remoto of accorgersi (si + accorse) meaning 'he became aware / noticed', 3rd person singular; the reflexive si is required by the verb. Word order places the locative and object before the verb for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-15-g23: O is the vocative interjection 'O/Oh'. Sanguis meus is a vocative phrase 'my blood' — a figurative, affectionate address meaning 'my offspring / my son'. Meus is the possessive 'my' agreeing with sanguis.
segments.paradiso-15-g24: The second vocative apposition: o = 'O'; superinfusa is a past participle/adjective 'poured over' or 'superinfused', modifying gratia; gratia Deï = 'grace of God' (Deï is the genitive). The phrase addresses either the person and/or the divine grace bestowed on him.
segments.paradiso-15-g25: Sicut = 'just as/like'. Tibi is a dative 'to you' (here introducing the person addressed), and cui is a relative/dative 'to whom' that refers back to that person; together tibi cui = 'to you, to whom...'. Bis 'twice' and unquam 'ever' modify ianüa reclusa ('gate opened'): reclusa is a past participle (feminine to agree with ianüa) from recludere meaning 'opened'. The whole line is a rhetorical/comparative question: 'as to you, to whom twice the gate of heaven was ever opened?' Note the archaic spelling ianüa with diaeresis marking syllabification.
segments.paradiso-15-g26: This segment is a single punctuation mark (a period) marking the end of the spoken question; no lexical items to analyze.
segments.paradiso-15-g27: Short nominative/topic phrase: 'Così' ('thus') modifies the following noun phrase; 'quel' is the demonstrative 'that'; 'lume' is the noun 'light' (here a guiding or luminous presence). The colon marks it as the subject/topic to which the following action relates.
segments.paradiso-15-g28: 'Ond’' is an archaic contraction of 'onde' meaning 'thereupon/therefore'; it introduces the immediate consequence. 'Io' is the explicit subject 'I'; 'm’' is the clitic reflexive/indirect object 'mi' attached to the verb; 'attesi' is the passato remoto of 'attendere' and here means 'applied myself/turned my attention' or 'awaited' in the sense 'I directed myself toward him'; 'a lui' is the prepositional complement 'to him.'
segments.paradiso-15-g29: 'Poscia' is an adverb 'afterwards/then.' 'Rivolsi' is passato remoto of 'rivolgere' meaning 'I turned/directed'; the verb takes a direct object 'il viso' and a prepositional indirect object 'a la mia donna' ('to my lady'). Word order places the indirect object before the direct object for emphasis but grammatically both complement the verb.
segments.paradiso-15-g30: Coordinated pair 'e quinci e quindi' = 'and from here and from there' (here/there = 'on this side and that side') using archaic adverbs; 'stupefatto' is a predicative past participle 'astonished'; 'fui' is passato remoto of 'essere' ('I was'), so the whole is 'and I was astonished on this side and on that.'
segments.paradiso-15-g31: 'Ché' = 'because/for' introduces the reason. 'Dentro a li occhi suoi' is a prepositional phrase 'within her eyes' (note archaic article 'li'); 'ardeva' is the imperfect 'was burning' of 'ardere' describing a continuing, vivid quality; 'un riso tal' places the indefinite noun 'a smile/laughter' after the verb, with 'tal' ('such') as an archaic modifier.
segments.paradiso-15-g32: The relative/result clause introduced by 'ch’' (= 'che') begins with 'io pensai' (passato remoto 'I thought'); what follows is an infinitive clause 'co’ miei toccar lo fondo' functioning as the object of 'pensai' ('I thought to touch'). 'Co’' is the contracted form of 'con i' meaning 'with the', here 'with my own'; 'miei' modifies the understood noun (elliptical 'my own [eyes/hands]'); 'toccar' is the infinitive 'to touch'; 'lo fondo' is 'the bottom/depth' (article + noun), and 'de la mia gloria e del mio paradiso' is a genitive phrase 'of my glory and of my paradise.'
segments.paradiso-15-g33: Indi is an adverb meaning 'then' or 'thereupon.' Placed at the clause start, it links this action temporally to what came before.
segments.paradiso-15-g34: The phrase a udire e a veder giocondo is a pre-verbal descriptor: a + infinitive ('to hear' / 'to see') expresses the activities that bring delight, and giocondo is an adjective modifying the (implied) subject that follows. Word order is poetic: the whole phrase qualifies the spirit as 'joyful to hear and to behold.'
segments.paradiso-15-g35: Giunse is passato remoto of giungere ('arrived/reached'); lo spirto is the subject with the definite article; al = a + il ('to the'); suo principio cose is a compressed, poetic genitival phrase meaning 'its principle/origin of things' (classical order: noun + qualifying noun).
segments.paradiso-15-g36: Ch’ = che ('that'); io non lo ’ntesi is a simple past statement: io (I) + non (not) + lo (it) + ’ntesi (past of intendere/ntendere) meaning 'I did not understand/hear it.' The clitic lo is the direct object referring back to what was said.
segments.paradiso-15-g37: Sì here is an adverb meaning 'so' or 'so much'; parlò is passato remoto 'spoke/uttered'; profondo is an adjective 'deep/profound.' Together they explain why the speaker did not understand: 'it spoke so profoundly.'
segments.paradiso-15-g38: Né introduces a negative continuation ('nor'); per elezïon means 'by choice/election' (elezione used in a theological/choice sense); mi si nascose is a reflexive construction meaning 'it did not hide itself from me' (mi = to me, si = reflexive), with nascose the passato remoto of nascondere.
segments.paradiso-15-g39: Ma per necessità is a short adversative clause: ma ('but'), per necessità ('by necessity' or 'out of necessity'), giving the reason why it did hide (i.e., not by choice).
segments.paradiso-15-g40: Ché = perché/for/because; ’l suo concetto = 'its concept/thought'; al segno d’i mortal si soprapuose is a poetic, passive/compound phrasing meaning 'was superimposed on the sign of mortal things' (al = to the, d’i mortal = of the mortals). Soprapuose is a past form meaning 'was placed above / overlaid.'
segments.paradiso-15-g41: E quando begins a temporal subordinate clause ('And when'); l’arco de l’ardente affetto is a metaphorical subject ('the bow of ardent affection'); fu is passato remoto ('was'); sì sfogato is a predicate (sì = 'so', sfogato = past participle 'vented/unstrung'), describing the state that lets speech descend.
segments.paradiso-15-g42: Che introduces a result clause ('so that'); ’l parlar is a nominalized infinitive 'the speaking/speech'; discese is passato remoto 'descended' and inver’ lo segno del nostro intelletto means 'toward / in the direction of the sign of our intellect' (inver' here = verso/toward).
segments.paradiso-15-g43: La prima cosa = 'the first thing'; che per me s’intese is a passive/reflexive perfect: per me ('to/for me') + s’intese (si + intese, past of intendere) meaning 'was understood/perceived by me.' The reflexive si makes the verb impersonal or passive in meaning.
segments.paradiso-15-g44: «Benedetto» is the past participle used adjectivally ('blessed'); sia is present subjunctive of essere used in a blessing/formula ('may you be' or 'be'); tu is the vocative/subject 'you.' Together they form the addressed blessing 'Blessed be you.'
segments.paradiso-15-g45: Fu is passato remoto 'was' and here links the reported utterance to being the first thing perceived: 'the first thing was "Blessed be you".'
segments.paradiso-15-g46: «Trino e uno» is a short apposition describing God as 'threefold and one' (Trinitarian formula): trino = 'threefold/trinitarian', e = 'and', uno = 'one.'
segments.paradiso-15-g47: Che introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('who/that'); nel mio seme = 'in my seed' (a metaphorical locution); se’ is the poetic contraction of sei ('you are') and tanto cortese = 'so gracious/noble.' The clause addresses the addressee's graciousness toward the speaker's 'seed.'
segments.paradiso-15-g48: This segment is a sentence-final punctuation mark (period) in the original — it closes the quoted exclamation. There are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.paradiso-15-g49: E seguì is a short narrative turn: E ('and') + seguì (passato remoto of seguire) meaning 'and he followed/continued' — here introducing the next spoken passage.
segments.paradiso-15-g50: «Grato e lontano digiuno» is a nominal phrase in vocative/appositive form: grato ('pleasing/grateful') and lontano ('distant/remote') modify digiuno ('fast' as a noun). The adjectives precede the noun in a poetic, elevated order.
segments.paradiso-15-g51: Tratto leggendo is a causal/participial phrase: tratto (past participle, 'drawn/taken') + leggendo (gerund 'by reading'), so 'drawn by reading'; del magno volume = 'from the great volume/book.' Du’ is a contracted/dialectal form meaning 'where' or 'in which' here (poetic du' = dove/di cui), and non si muta mai bianco né bruno means 'neither white nor dark ever changes' — i.e., the book's record does not change color (metaphorically: the entries are fixed).
segments.paradiso-15-g52: Solvuto hai is a perfect construction where the past participle solvuto ('paid/fulfilled/loosened') precedes the auxiliary hai ('you have') — a syntactic inversion common in poetry — and it means 'you have fulfilled/paid [it].'
segments.paradiso-15-g53: Figlio is a vocative 'son' addressing the addressee directly; it stands alone as an affectionate or filial address.
segments.paradiso-15-g54: Dentro a questo lume = 'within this light' (dentro a = inside/within); in ch’io ti parlo is a relative clause 'in which I speak to you' (ch’ = che = 'which/that', io = I, ti = to you, parlo = I speak).
segments.paradiso-15-g55: Mercè di colei = 'by the mercy/favor of her' (mercè used as 'grace/favor'); ch’a l’alto volo ti vestì le piume is a relative clause: ch’ = che ('who'), a l’alto volo = 'for/to the high flight', ti vestì (vestì = past of vestire) = 'clothed you', le piume = 'the feathers' (i.e., clothed you with feathers/wings). The whole gives the credited cause: thanks to her who clothed you for the high flight.
segments.paradiso-15-g115: Non + aveva = negative + imperfect of avere, meaning 'did not have' or 'had not'. 'case di famiglia' is a noun phrase 'houses of the family / ancestral houses' (famiglia as possessor), and 'vòte' is an archaic spelling of 'vuote' (empty), an adjective agreeing with 'case' placed after the noun for metrical/poetic effect.
segments.paradiso-15-g116: The clause has inverted subject order: 'non v'era giunto ancor Sardanapalo' = 'Sardanapalus had not yet arrived there'; 'v’' is the contracted 'vi' meaning 'there'. 'era giunto' uses essere + past participle (giunto) because 'giungere' is intransitive — here a perfect/imperfect sense 'had arrived' or 'was arrived'. 'a mostrar' is an infinitive of purpose 'to show'. 'ciò che ’n camera si puote' = 'what in the chamber one can / is possible' where '’n' is the poetic contraction of 'in' and 'si puote' is impersonal reflexive/impersonal passive meaning 'one can / it is possible'.
segments.paradiso-15-g117: Non era vinto ancora = 'had not yet been defeated/overcome' (era + past participle = imperfect passive). 'Montemalo' is the grammatical subject placed after the verb (inversion common in poetry). 'dal vostro Uccellatoio' names the agent using dal (di + il) meaning 'by the / from the', here 'by your Uccellatoio' (an aviary or bird-trap).
segments.paradiso-15-g118: The single word 'che' is a relative pronoun ('which/that') introducing the subordinate clause that follows; it links the previous clause to the explanatory comparison.
segments.paradiso-15-g119: com' è vinto nel montar sù = 'as it is overcome in the mounting up / on the ascent'. 'com'' is the contracted 'come' (as/just as). 'è vinto' is present passive 'is overcome' used to state a general fact, and 'nel montar sù' is 'in the act of mounting/ascending' ('nel' + infinitive used nominally, with the adverb 'sù' = up).
segments.paradiso-15-g120: così sarà nel calo = 'thus it will be in the descent'. 'così' = thus/so; 'sarà' is future indicative 'will be'; 'nel calo' = 'in the falling/decline/descent' (parallel to 'nel montar sù'). The clause predicts a mirrored result on the way down.
segments.paradiso-15-g121: Bellincion Berti vid' io = inversion where 'vid' (archaic contraction of vidi) + subject 'io' means 'I saw'. 'andar cinto di cuoio e d' osso' = 'going girded/encircled with leather and bone' where 'cinto' is a participle/adjectival past participle describing his state while 'andar' (infinitive) describes the motion seen. Poetic contractions (vid' for vidi, d' for di) are common.
segments.paradiso-15-g122: This continues the list of things seen: 'e venir da lo specchio la donna sua' = 'and his woman came from the mirror'. Word order places the noun phrase after the infinitive for poetic rhythm. 'sanza 'l viso dipinto' = 'without the face painted' (sanza = without). 'dipinto' is an adjective/participial adjective 'painted' (i.e., wearing painted makeup), here meaning 'unpainted' as she comes without painted face.
segments.paradiso-15-g123: Again 'e vidi' = 'and I saw'. 'quel d'i Nerli e quel del Vecchio' are elliptical identifiers meaning 'the one of the Nerli family and the one of (the house called) the Vecchio'. 'esser contenti' is the infinitive 'to be content' used as the object of 'vidi' (I saw them to be content). 'a la pelle scoperta' is a prepositional phrase 'with their skin uncovered' indicating manner.
segments.paradiso-15-g124: 'e le sue donne al fuso e al pennecchio' = 'and his women at the spindle and at the distaff' (women occupied with spinning). 'al' = a + il, introducing the instruments; 'fuso' = spindle, 'pennecchio' is the distaff-related tool (here a term for the partner implement), both objects of the preposition indicating their activity or place.
segments.paradiso-15-g125: An exclamatory vocative: 'Oh fortunate!' addresses those just described. 'Fortunate' is a poetic vocative/adjective meaning 'fortunate' or 'blessed' (plural implied by context); the interjection 'Oh' marks exclamation.
segments.paradiso-15-g99: Poscia is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'then' or 'afterwards.' Mi is the indirect object pronoun 'to me.' Disse is the passato remoto (simple past) of dire, 'said,' and takes mi as the person spoken to (disse a me).
segments.paradiso-15-g100: Quel ("that one") begins a relative construction. 'da cui si dice tua cognazione' is a passive/impersonal construction: si dice = 'it is said' / 'they say,' with da cui 'from whom' linking the remark to that person and tua cognazione as the predicate ('said to be your kin'). The coordinating e introduces another relative clause 'che ... girato ha,' where girato ha is a poetic inversion of the compound perfect (participial form before the auxiliary) meaning 'has gone/turned,' and 'cent’ anni e piùe' = 'a hundred years and more'; 'in la prima cornice' = 'in the first ring/circle.'
segments.paradiso-15-g101: Two balanced clauses with the past historic (fu, fue) state the kinship: 'mio figlio fu' = 'was my son' and, coordinated by e, 'tuo bisavol fue' = 'was your great‑grandfather.' The repeated past forms are poetic and equivalent to modern fu/fu; bisavol is an archaic form of 'great‑grandfather' or ancestor.
segments.paradiso-15-g102: Ben convien (convien = impersonal verb, 'it is fitting/necessary') is reinforced by ben. Che introduces a subordinate clause with the present subjunctive (raccorci) expressing what ought to be done: 'tu li raccorci' = 'you shorten them/it for him' (li is an old pronoun usage referring back to the burden/years), and con l’opere tue = 'by/with your works' (opere = your deeds/actions). Word order places the impersonal main clause before the subordinate that contains the agent tu.
segments.paradiso-15-g103: Fiorenza is the feminine proper name 'Florence.' Dentro da la cerchia antica uses dentro + da (a somewhat redundant/poetic prepositional phrase) to mean 'within/from the ancient circuit' (cerchia antica = the old city walls or enclosure). The article la agrees with cerchia.
segments.paradiso-15-g104: Ond' is the contracted form of 'onde' meaning 'whence' or 'from which.' Ella (the city, treated as feminine) is the subject and toglie (present indicative of togliere) here means 'takes' or 'derives' (i.e., takes its character or name). Ancora = 'still,' and the correlative e ... e = 'both ... and' links terza and nona (the third and the ninth).
segments.paradiso-15-g105: Si stava is a reflexive/impersonal use of stare in the imperfect: 'si stava in pace' = 'it/one/itself/it (the city) stood/was in peace.' Stava is the imperfect tense describing a continuing state.
segments.paradiso-15-g106: Two predicate adjectives in feminine form agree with Fiorenza: sobria = 'restrained/modest/plain' and pudica = 'chaste/modest.' They describe the city's moral/visual character.
segments.paradiso-15-g107: Non avea is the imperfect 'had not' (negated possession). Catenella is a diminutive noun 'little chain' (a small necklace), the direct object of avea.
segments.paradiso-15-g108: Another short negative noun phrase in parallel: non corona = 'no crown/wreath.' The ellipsis of a verb is allowed because it repeats from the previous clause (she had no ...).
segments.paradiso-15-g109: Non gonne contigiate: gonne is plural 'skirts' or 'robes' and contigiate is an archaic adjective; grammatically this is another item in the negative list (she had no ...). Word order is noun after negation as before. The precise meaning of contigiate is archaic/uncertain in the supplied senses and here likely implies decorated/stitched, but that sense is not in the provided list.
segments.paradiso-15-g110: Non cintura = 'no belt/girdle.' The relative clause introduced by che + imperf. subj. (fosse) describes a belt 'that was for showing more than the person': che fosse a veder = 'that would be to be seen' or 'intended to be seen,' and più che la persona is a comparative 'more than the person' (i.e., the belt shown more than the wearer). The subjunctive fosse reflects the hypothetical/relative/descriptive clause.
segments.paradiso-15-g111: Non faceva is imperfect of fare with negation: here 'faceva (paura)' = 'caused fear' (idiom: fare paura = to frighten), so Non faceva = 'did not cause.' The verb is descriptive of a habitual or ongoing circumstance at birth.
segments.paradiso-15-g112: Nascendo is the present participle/adverbial gerund 'being born' or 'at birth' and provides the circumstantial time frame for the main clause (when she was born).
segments.paradiso-15-g113: This order is inverted for metrical reasons: 'ancor paura la figlia al padre' = 'the daughter did not yet cause fear to the father.' Ancor = 'yet/still,' paura = 'fear' (object), la figlia = subject, and al padre = indirect object 'to the father.'
segments.paradiso-15-g114: Che introduces an explanatory clause ('for/since'). '’l tempo e la dote' = 'time and the dowry.' Non fuggien is an archaic 3rd‑plural form of fuggire ('do not flee/escape'); quinci e quindi = 'on this side and on that' (here and there); la misura = 'the measure' (i.e., due moderation). Grammatically, the clause says time and dowry do not escape proper measure, which explains why the father had no fear.
segments.paradiso-15-g56: Tu (subject) + credi (2nd‑person present indicative) introduces a that‑clause (che). a me marks the indirect object 'to me'. tuo pensier is the noun phrase 'your thought'. The word mei is an archaic form; here it appears inside the noun phrase and is best read as an older/poetic form related to possession or emphasis tied into the expression ('my/own' in the text's morphology) that does not change the clause structure. da quel ch’è primo begins a prepositional phrase 'from that which is First' with ch’ as the relative/that and è (present) + primo.
segments.paradiso-15-g57: così come is a comparative 'just as'. raia is used in the sense 'ray' (a beam or radiation) and da l’un means 'from the One' (the ultimate source). The construction compares the flow of Dante's thought to a ray issuing from a single source.
segments.paradiso-15-g58: se introduces a conditional 'if'. si is the impersonal/reflective particle (used to form a general 'if one/it is known'), and conosce is present (understood impersonally here as 'is known' or 'one knows').
segments.paradiso-15-g59: il cinque e ’l sei are the literal numerals 'the five and the six' used here as things that are understood to issue (metaphorically) from the One in the preceding simile; the article il/l marks them as definite items.
segments.paradiso-15-g60: e però ch’ introduces a causal clause 'and because'. io mi sia uses the present subjunctive of essere (sia) with the reflexive clitic mi — a subjunctive used here to state the speaker's being/existence in the mode of appearance; e perch’ io paia repeats the causal idea 'and because I may seem' using the present subjunctive paia 'may appear' to indicate appearance rather than assertion. più gaudïoso a te = 'more joyful to you' (più + adjective + a + person).
segments.paradiso-15-g61: non negates the verb; mi is the indirect object 'of me' (or clitic relating to the verb), and domandi is 2nd‑person present subjunctive/imperative sense here as 'ask' — 'do not ask me'.
segments.paradiso-15-g62: che begins the subordinate clause (here 'whether/that'); alcun altro = 'any/anyone else' (alcun in the sense 'any'), in questa turba gaia = 'in this joyous crowd/throng'. The clause completes the prohibition 'do not ask me whether any other is in this joyful company.'
segments.paradiso-15-g63: Simple declarative: Tu (subject) + credi (2nd‑person present) + ’l vero (contracted il vero, 'the truth'). Here credi = 'you are right/you believe'.
segments.paradiso-15-g64: ché = 'for/because' introducing the reason. i minori e ’ grandi = 'the lesser and the greater' (people of lower and higher rank) and di questa vita = 'of this life'. miran (3rd plural) = 'gaze/look' and ne lo speglio = 'into the mirror' (ne = in/into + lo speglio 'the mirror'); in che = 'in which' introduces the relative clause.
segments.paradiso-15-g65: prima che introduces a temporal clause 'before' and governs the subjunctive; pensi is 2nd‑person present subjunctive 'you think' because 'prima che' requires the subjunctive in Dante's usage: 'before you think.'
segments.paradiso-15-g66: il pensier = 'the thought' (direct object of the implied verb). pandi is an archaic form (from pandere) meaning 'you spread/open' (2nd person singular present), so the clause means that in that mirror you lay the thought open before you even think it.
segments.paradiso-15-g67: ma perché = 'but/because'; 'l sacro amore = 'the sacred love'; in che io veglio = 'in which I keep vigil/watch' (veglio = present of vegliare). con perpetüa vista = 'with perpetual sight/vision' and che m’asseta di dolce disïar = 'and which satisfies me with sweet desire', where m’ is the clitic for mi and asseta = 'satisfies/sets at rest'.
segments.paradiso-15-g68: s’adempia meglio = si adempia meglio; si is the impersonal/reflexive particle and adempia (present/subjunctive/indicative as used here) means 'is fulfilled' or 'is completed' and meglio 'better' — 'is better fulfilled/completed.'
segments.paradiso-15-g69: la voce tua sicura = 'your sure/secure voice' (la voce + possessive tua + adjective sicura). This is the noun phrase that the following verb will apply to.
segments.paradiso-15-g70: balda e lieta are two coordinating adjectives 'bold and glad' modifying la voce; suoni is present subjunctive of sonare used optatively/jussively here ('may sound' / 'let sound') and la volontà is the direct object 'the will' (i.e. let your voice sound to the will).
segments.paradiso-15-g71: suoni again is an optative subjunctive 'let ... sound' and ’l disio = 'the desire/longing' (elided article l' + disio) is the thing that should sound; the repetition stresses both will and desire.
segments.paradiso-15-g72: a che = 'to which/whereby' introducing the result clause; la mia risposta = 'my answer/response'; è già decreta is present indicative of essere + the participial adjective decreta ('is already decreed/has already been decided'), i.e. 'my answer is already determined.'
segments.paradiso-15-g73: A single period marking the end of the quotation/sentence.
segments.paradiso-15-g74: Io (subject) + mi volsi is passato remoto (volsi) of volgersi/volgere with reflexive clitic mi: 'I turned (myself) to Beatrice.' a introduces the indirect object/target (Beatrice).
segments.paradiso-15-g75: e quella udio: quella 'that one' (Beatrice) + udio (archaic orthography of udì) is 3rd‑person singular passato remoto 'she heard'. pria ch’io parlassi = 'before I spoke' with parlassi in the subjunctive because of 'pria che' ('before').
segments.paradiso-15-g76: e arrisemi un cenno: arrisemi is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'gave/caused to reach me' (here 'gave me') + un cenno 'a gesture/a sign'. che fece crescer l’ali al voler mio is a relative clause: che = 'which', fece (passato remoto of fare) = 'made/caused', crescer (infinitive) = 'to grow', l’ali = 'the wings', al voler mio = 'to my will/wish' (al = a + il).
segments.paradiso-15-g1: Benigna volontade is the noun phrase subject ('benign/friendly will'). In che introduces a relative clause ('in which'); si is a reflexive pronoun attached to liqua, and liqua (present form of liquare) means 'to melt/liquefy' here (the reflexive construction 'si liqua' = 'is liquefied' or 'melts itself'). Sempre is an adverb modifying the verb and comes before the subject and verb cluster l’amor ... spira; the relative clause che drittamente spira modifies amor ('the love which breathes straight/directly').
segments.paradiso-15-g2: Come introduces a simile ('as'), and cupidità here denotes an intense desire or avarice acting like a force; fa is the present of fare ('makes/does') taking an implicit object (it 'makes' the same effect in the unjust); ne la iniqua is a poetic word order meaning 'in the unjust' (ne = in, la = the, iniqua = unjust).
segments.paradiso-15-g3: Silenzio is the subject ('silence'); puose is passato remoto of porre/ponere ('placed/laid'); a marks the indirect relation 'upon' (a quella lira = on that lyre), and quella dolce lira is the object ('that sweet lyre'). The overall image: silence was placed upon the lyre.
segments.paradiso-15-g4: E fece is another passato remoto causative ('and it made/caused'); quïetar is the infinitive 'to quiet/silence' used with fece in a causative construction ('caused ... to be quiet'); le sante corde is the direct object (the holy strings). The relative clause che la destra del cielo allenta e tira modifies corde: 'which the right hand of heaven slackens and pulls' — allenta and tira are present-tense actions attributed to that heavenly right hand.
segments.paradiso-15-g5: Come saranno is a rhetorical interrogative ('how will ... be' / 'how must ... be'); a’ giusti preghi is an indirect-object phrase 'to the prayers of the just' (a + article); sorde is a predicate adjective agreeing with quelle sustanze (meaning 'deaf' or 'unheeding'); quelle sustanze is the noun phrase the relative clause that follows refers to.
segments.paradiso-15-g6: Per darmi voglia is a purpose/result phrase 'to give me a desire'; darmi is an infinitive with enclitic mi ('to give to me'); voglia is 'desire' or 'inclination'. Ch’io le pregassi is a subordinate clause with ch’ = che introducing a subjunctive (pregassi is imperfect subjunctive) expressing the desired potential/result ('that I might pray to them'); le is the clitic pronoun referring back to quelle sustanze.
segments.paradiso-15-g7: A tacer is the infinitive phrase 'to be silent' (tacer = tace­re), used here after per ... voglia to express the intended state; fur is the archaic past (passato remoto) plural 'they were' (from essere), and concorde ('in agreement') functions as a predicate: 'they were of one mind to be silent.' The inverted order (a tacer fur concorde) is poetic.
segments.paradiso-15-g8: Bene è is an impersonal evaluative construction ('it is good/right'); che introduces a relative clause. Sanza termine is an adverbial phrase 'without end'; si doglia is reflexive ('he grieves' / 'is in pain') with doglia used intransitively; chi is the relative/indefinite 'who' ('he who').
segments.paradiso-15-g9: Per amor di cosa introduces the reason/purpose 'for love of a thing'; che non duri is a subordinate relative clause where duri is present subjunctive ('that does not last') used because the clause characterizes an indefinite/unsure thing; eternalmente is the adverb 'eternally'.
segments.paradiso-15-g10: Quello amor is the resumptive subject 'that love' (quello = 'that one'); si spoglia is reflexive present indicative 'strips itself' or 'is stripped' — idiomatically 'renounces/loses that love'. The reflexive makes the action reflexive/intransitive.
segments.paradiso-15-g11: Quale opens a comparative simile ('such as' / 'just as'); per li seren tranquilli e puri is an ablative/locative phrase 'through the serene, tranquil and pure (heavens)'; discorre is a present-tense verb here meaning 'passes/runs through' (poetic use); ad ora ad or is a repetitive adverbial ('from hour to hour' / 'now at one hour now at another'), and sùbito foco is the noun phrase 'sudden fire' that moves through the heavens.
segments.paradiso-15-g12: Movendo is a gerund ('moving') that links to the previous clause; li occhi ('the eyes') is the object being moved; che stavan sicuri is a relative clause with stavan (imperfect of stare) 'were remaining/stood' and sicuri 'steady/secure' describing eyes formerly fixed.
segments.paradiso-15-g13: E pare stella introduces the image 'and it appears a star'; che tramuti loco is a relative clause in which tramuti (present subjunctive/poetic present) means 'changes/moves' place (tramuta = change/turn), so the fire seems like a star changing its position.
segments.paradiso-15-g14: Se non che is an idiom meaning 'except that'; da la parte ond’ e’ s’accende gives the source 'from the side whence it is kindled' (ond' = whence), nulla ... sen perde states 'nothing of it is lost' — sen is an enclitic (se + ne) meaning 'of it/away', and perde is present indicative 'loses'.
segments.paradiso-15-g15: Ed esso dura poco is a short independent clause 'and it endures little' (esso = it, dura = lasts/endures, poco = little/briefly); dura is present indicative describing the short duration of that phenomenon.
segments.paradiso-15-g16: Tale begins the second half of the simile ('such ...'), and dal corno che ’n destro si stende a piè di quella croce is a descriptive locator 'from the horn that stretches to the right at the foot of that cross'; corse is passato remoto of correre ('ran/shot forth') and governs un astro de la costellazion che lì resplende ('a star of the constellation that shines there').
segments.paradiso-15-g17: Né si partì la gemma dal suo nastro: né 'nor'; si partì is a reflexive/passive past ('did not part/separate itself'); la gemma is the subject 'the gem'; dal suo nastro 'from its ribbon' — the line says the gem did not detach from its ribbon.
segments.paradiso-15-g18: Ma per la lista radïal trascorse: ma 'but'; per indicates motion 'along/through' the lista radïal (a radiating strip/ribbon); trascorse is passato remoto of trascorrere ('ran/passed along'), so the gem/star traveled along the radial band rather than leaving its ribbon.
segments.paradiso-15-g19: Che parve foco dietro ad alabastro is a relative clause modifying lista radïal ('which seemed fire behind alabaster'): parve is passato remoto of parere ('seemed'), foco is 'fire', and dietro ad alabastro gives the visual simile 'as if fire behind alabaster (a milky stone)'.
segments.paradiso-15-g146: Moronto is a proper name (subject). 'fu' is the passato remoto of essere, meaning 'was'. 'mio frate' places a possessive before the noun: 'my brother' (frate here = brother, not necessarily a friar). 'ed' is the form of 'e' used before a vowel and simply means 'and'. 'Eliseo' is another name placed in apposition to 'mio frate'. The clause is a simple past copular clause: [Name] + was + [NP].
segments.paradiso-15-g147: 'mia donna' is literally 'my lady' (a possessive + feminine noun; in literary register it can mean a beloved or noble lady). 'venne' is the passato remoto of venire, 'came'. The prepositional phrase 'a me' marks direction ('to me'). 'di val di Pado' uses two genitive/of-prepositions: 'di val' = 'from/of the valley' and 'di Pado' = 'of the Po' (Pado = the river Po); here 'di' before 'val' is best read as 'from' or indicating origin.
segments.paradiso-15-g148: 'e' = 'and'. 'quindi' here means 'from that' / 'thereupon' (introducing the result). 'il sopranome tuo' = 'your nickname/surname' (definite article + noun + possessive). 'si feo' is an antiquated passive/medio construction: 'si' is the reflexive/passive clitic and 'feo' is the passato remoto of fare in a passive sense, so 'si feo' = 'was made / came to be'. The clause therefore states the result derived from the previous clause.
segments.paradiso-15-g149: 'Poi' = 'then' / 'afterwards'. 'seguitai' is the passato remoto of seguire, 'I followed'. 'lo 'mperador' is 'il imperador' with apostrophe-marking elision (the article + emperor); 'Currado' is the proper name (Conrad). The whole clause is a simple narrative past: 'Then I followed Emperor Conrad.'
segments.paradiso-15-g150: 'ed' = 'and'. 'el' is an older form of 'egli/il' here = 'he'. 'mi' is the clitic object 'me'. 'cinse' is the passato remoto of cingere (to gird, to invest, to encircle) so 'he girded/encircled me'. 'de' is an archaic form of 'di' meaning 'with/of' depending on context; 'la sua milizia' = 'his militia/host/forces' — together: 'and he invested me with his troops.'
segments.paradiso-15-g151: 'tanto' here introduces a degree/result clause: 'so (much/that)'. 'per bene ovrar' is a purpose/means phrase: 'per' = 'for' (for the sake of / by means of), 'bene' = 'good/well', 'ovrar' is an archaic infinitive (ovrare) 'to do/perform (works)'. 'li' is a clitic referring back to the group (them/that body). 'venni' (passato remoto of venire) = 'I came' or 'I attained'. 'in grado' = 'into rank/position' (in + grado). Altogether: 'so (that), by doing good deeds, I came into rank among them.'
segments.paradiso-15-g152: 'Dietro' with a pronoun 'li' here means 'after them' / 'behind them'. 'andai' is passato remoto of andare: 'I went'. 'incontro a' is a verbal phrase meaning 'to go to meet/encounter/oppose'. 'la nequizia' = 'the wickedness/corruption'. The phrase continues with a relative clause 'di quella legge il cui popolo usurpa' = 'of that law whose people usurp' — 'il cui' = 'whose' modifies 'legge', and 'popolo usurpa' is a present-tense clause 'its people usurp'.
segments.paradiso-15-g153: 'per colpa d’i pastor' = 'through the fault of the shepherds'. 'per colpa di' is a fixed expression meaning 'by/in consequence of the fault/guilt of'. 'd’i' is the contracted form of 'di + i' (of the). 'pastor' = 'shepherds' (can be literal or spiritual pastors).
segments.paradiso-15-g154: 'vostra' = the second-person plural possessive 'your' (addressing the addressee). 'giustizia' = 'justice' (here it can mean legal/administrative justice or justice in a broader sense); the whole noun phrase is the object of the earlier clause ('they usurp your justice').
segments.paradiso-15-g155: 'Quivi' = 'there' (locative adverb). 'fu' (passato remoto of essere) + 'io' = 'I was'. 'da quella gente turpa' is an agent/ablative phrase 'by/from that shameful people' (da can indicate agent with passive sense). 'disviluppato' is an archaic participle meaning roughly 'unfolded', 'divested', or 'freed/stripped' (here used in a passive sense with 'fu'), and 'dal mondo fallace' = 'from the deceitful/false world' (dal = da + il). The clause reads as a past passive/result: 'There I was, by that shameful people disviluppato from the deceitful world.'
segments.paradiso-15-g156: 'lo cui' is a literary combination where 'lo' (the) precedes 'cui' (whose) — here 'lo' points back to the preceding masculine noun (mondo or the general idea just expressed). 'amor' = 'love' (the object/force associated with that world). 'molt’' = shortened 'molte' or 'molte' in archaic spelling: 'many'. 'anime' = 'souls'. 'deturpa' is present-tense (indicative) of deturpare meaning 'spoils/defiles/sullies'. Grammatically the relative clause attributes to that world (or its love) the power of corrupting many souls.
segments.paradiso-15-g157: 'e' = 'and'. 'venni' is the passato remoto of venire, 'I came'. 'dal martiro' = 'from the martyrdom / from the torment' (dal = da + il). 'a questa pace' = 'to this peace' (direction/result). The clause narrates movement from suffering (martyrdom) to the present state of peace.
segments.paradiso-15-g126: 'Ciascuna' (feminine singular) = 'each one'; it is the subject. 'era' is the imperfect indicative of essere ('was'), a simple past/descriptive tense. 'certa' is an adjective agreeing with the subject meaning 'certain/sure'. 'de la sepultura' is a prepositional genitive ('of the grave/tomb'); the whole phrase means 'each one was sure of its tomb.' Word order is subject + verb + adjective + prepositional complement (typical in poetic Italian).
segments.paradiso-15-g127: 'e' = 'and' linking the second clause. 'ancor' (archaic form of 'ancora') = 'still/yet'. 'nulla' = 'nothing' (here used impersonally: 'no [place/person]'). 'era' again is imperfect 'was'. 'per Francia' uses 'per' with a territorial sense ('through/over/in France'). 'nel letto' = 'in the bed' (letto here is literal 'bed' or a geographic/poetic 'bed' image); 'diserta' (agrees with 'letto') is an adjective meaning 'deserted/abandoned'. The clause means roughly 'and as yet no bed/place in France lay deserted.'
segments.paradiso-15-g128: 'L'una' = 'the one' (feminine) functions as the subject; the apostrophe shows elision. 'vegghiava' is the imperfect of the older verb 'vegghiare' ('was keeping watch/guarding'), describing an ongoing past action. 'a studio' uses 'a' + 'studio' where 'studio' means 'zeal/attentive care' (not a literal study room). 'de la culla' is a genitive 'of the cradle' (the object of the watch).
segments.paradiso-15-g129: Single coordinating conjunction 'e' = 'and', linking the gerundial phrase that follows to the previous clause.
segments.paradiso-15-g130: 'Consolando' is a present participle/gerund ('comforting' or 'while consoling') that gives the manner of the action and attaches to the same subject ('L'una'). It's circumstantial (adverbial) and indicates simultaneous action with the main verb that follows.
segments.paradiso-15-g131: 'Usava' is the imperfect of 'usare' and here expresses habitual or customary past action ('used/was accustomed to use'). 'l'idïoma' is the direct object (the language/dialect). The relative pronoun 'che' introduces a clause that characterizes that idiom; within that relative clause 'prima' = 'formerly', and 'i padri e le madri' are the logical agents in the clause. The verb 'trastulla' (3rd person singular present) takes as its subject the singular 'idïoma' (so: 'the idiom that formerly amused/entertained fathers and mothers'). Note the mix of imperfect in the main clause and present in the relative clause is stylistic and normal in older/poetic Italian.
segments.paradiso-15-g132: 'L'altra' = 'the other (woman)'; feminine singular, introducing the contrasting subject. The comma marks the shift to the second woman's action.
segments.paradiso-15-g133: 'Traendo' is a present participle of 'traire' (to draw/bring) used adverbially ('drawing/gathering'). It modifies the main verb of the clause (favoleggiava) and describes a concurrent action. 'a la rocca' = 'toward/to the fortress' (a + definite article), and 'la chioma' = 'the hair' (direct object of 'traendo' — 'drawing her hair up/toward the rocca').
segments.paradiso-15-g134: 'Favoleggiava' is the imperfect of 'favoleggiare' ('was telling tales/relating fables'), a descriptive past tense. 'Con la sua famiglia' = 'with her household/household members' (an accompaniment/comitative phrase). 'd'i Troiani' ('of the Trojans') qualifies the family as of Trojan descent or their stories as about the Trojans; 'di' indicates origin/association.
segments.paradiso-15-g135: A simple coordination of prepositional phrases naming sources/subjects of the tales: 'di Fiesole e di Roma' = 'of Fiesole and of Rome.' 'di' marks origin/association; the nouns are proper place names.
segments.paradiso-15-g136: 'Saria' is the conditional of 'essere' ('would be'). 'tenuta' is the past participle of 'tenere' used here passively ('held' or better 'considered/regarded'). Together 'saria tenuta' = 'would be considered/held to be.' 'allor' = 'then' (temporal adverb). 'tal maraviglia' = 'such a marvel'; 'una Cianghella' uses the indefinite article with a personal name or nickname, treating it as a common example ('a Cianghella'). Grammatically this is a conditional/predictive construction evaluating social esteem.
segments.paradiso-15-g137: Apposition listing another lowly/personified example: 'un Lapo Salterello'—'un' is the indefinite article and 'Lapo' a proper name; 'Salterello' is a nickname or epithet (here presented as a common-noun epithet). The phrase hangs as a second example of who would be so regarded.
segments.paradiso-15-g138: 'Qual' (here 'such as'/'which') introduces the comparison to the present: 'or' = 'now' (an adverb). 'saria' again conditional 'would be'. The two proper names 'Cincinnato e Corniglia' function as standards of esteem; the clause means 'which now would be (taken as) Cincinnatus and Corniglia' (i.e., today those latter names are the ones regarded as wonders).
segments.paradiso-15-g139: 'A così riposato' is a prepositional phrase where 'A' functions like 'to/at' introducing the state or condition; 'così' 'so/thus' qualifies 'riposato' (past participle used as noun/adjective 'rest' or 'being rested'). It sets up one of several parallel phrases describing the state that follows.
segments.paradiso-15-g140: Another parallel phrase: 'a così bello viver di cittadini' — 'a' again introduces, 'così bello' qualifies 'viver' (a substantive infinitive 'living' = 'life'), and 'di cittadini' is a genitive 'of citizens' (i.e., civic life). The order is idiomatic poetic word order.
segments.paradiso-15-g141: Continues the series: 'a così fida cittadinanza' uses 'fida' (adjective 'faithful') modifying 'cittadinanza' ('citizenship' or 'citizenry'); same introductory 'a così' pattern for rhetorical accumulation.
segments.paradiso-15-g142: Another parallel nominal phrase: 'a così dolce ostello' — 'dolce' ('sweet') modifies 'ostello' ('lodging/hostel'/'refuge'), continuing the catalogue of blessings. The repetition of the prepositional 'a così' is a stylistic anaphora.
segments.paradiso-15-g143: 'Maria mi diè' is a simple clause: 'Maria' (subject) + clitic 'mi' ('to me') + 'diè' (passato remoto of 'dare' = 'gave'). The short clause states that Mary bestowed something on the speaker; the precise object is supplied by context or the preceding enumerations.
segments.paradiso-15-g144: 'Chiamata in alte grida' is a participial phrase modifying the action of the previous clause: 'chiamata' (past participle of 'chiamare') here = 'having been called/summoned'; 'in alte grida' specifies by what means — 'in high/loud cries' (an idiom for being called aloud). Word order places the participle after the main clause for poetic effect.
segments.paradiso-15-g145: 'E ne l’antico vostro Batisteo insieme fui cristiano e Cacciaguida' is the concluding clause. 'Ne' here functions as 'in' (ne + l' = 'in the'); 'antico vostro Batisteo' is the old/ancient place (archaic name/term); 'insieme fui' = 'I was together/at once' with 'cristiano e Cacciaguida' as predicative complements (he was both a Christian and named Cacciaguida). 'Fui' is passato remoto 'I was.'
segments.paradiso-15-g77: Poi is an adverb meaning "then/afterwards." Cominciai is the first-person singular passato remoto of cominciare ("I began"). Così is an adverb "thus/so" pointing forward to the direct speech that follows; the colon introduces the quoted words.
segments.paradiso-15-g78: L’ is the elided definite article (il) before a vowel. Affetto is the noun "affection/will/feeling" and, with l’ before it, forms the first term of the compound subject. E is the coordinating conjunction "and." L (another elided article) + senno ("reason/wisdom") form the second term of the subject; together they name what performs the action in the reported clause.
segments.paradiso-15-g79: Come here is a comparative conjunction meaning "as." La prima equalità is the noun phrase "the first equality/first likeness," with prima qualifying equalità. V’ is the clitic form of vi meaning "to you" and is placed before the verb. Appar­se is the passato remoto (3rd sing.) of apparire, used here with the clitic to yield "appeared to you."
segments.paradiso-15-g80: D’un is the contraction of di + un and introduces the complement "of a/an." Peso is a noun "weight/burden." Per ciascun di voi is a prepositional phrase "for each of you." Si + fenno is a reflexive/passive-like construction: si is the reflexive clitic and fenno is an archaic passato remoto (3rd pl.) form meaning "they made/they became"—here best rendered "were made" or "became" referring back to the two terms of the subject.
segments.paradiso-15-g81: Però che introduces the reason/result clause here as "because/for that". 'L sol is the poetic form for "the sun" (here the celestial sun that acted on them). The relative che links to 'sol' and v’ (vi) is the clitic "you"; allumò is passato remoto "lit up/kindled" and arse is passato remoto of ardere "burnt/consumed." Both verbs share 'il sol' as subject.
segments.paradiso-15-g82: Col is the contraction of con + il, "with the." Caldo is a noun here meaning "heat." E is "and," con la luce repeats the preposition+article+noun "with the light." È is the present indicative of essere "is," and sì is an adverb "so/so much." Igual­i is the adjective "equal/similar." The clause states that in both heat and light the sun is so equal (i.e., distributes them equally).
segments.paradiso-15-g83: Che introduces the result clause "that/so that." Tutte simiglianze is the subject phrase "all resemblances/comparisons." Sono is present indicative "are," and scarse is the predicate adjective "scant/insufficient." Together the clause means that any likenesses fall short (are inadequate).
segments.paradiso-15-g84: Ma is the adversative conjunction "but." Voglia means "desire/will," and argomento here means "argument/reasoning" or the intellectual faculty; the two form the compound subject. Ne’ mortali uses ne ("in/among") with the plural mortali ("mortals/humankind") to locate these faculties among humans.
segments.paradiso-15-g85: Per la cagion means "for the reason/because"; ch’ is the relative/connector "that." A voi (to you) is a dative clitic phrase indicating the reason is evident to the addressees; è manifesta is the present indicative "is manifest/clear."
segments.paradiso-15-g86: Diversamente means "differently." Son is the contracted form of sono (3rd pl.) agreeing with the plural subject "voglia e argomento." Pennuti is an adjective meaning "feathered/winged" (poetic image); in ali literally "in wings" indicates that among mortals these two faculties are differently "winged"—i.e., differ in how they are expressed or developed.
segments.paradiso-15-g87: Ond’ is a contracted form of onde/ond ("whence/therefore/wherefore") introducing the speaker's consequence; io is the emphatic first-person pronoun "I."
segments.paradiso-15-g88: Che is the relative pronoun "who/which" referring to the speaker; son is the singular form of essere used with io here ("I am")—an archaic son for sono; mortal is the adjective "mortal/human."
segments.paradiso-15-g89: Mi sento is a reflexive present-tense construction "I feel myself" or "I feel." In questa disagguaglianza is a prepositional phrase "in this inequality/difference," completing the thought "I feel myself in this inequality."
segments.paradiso-15-g90: E però links the consequence "and therefore." Non ringrazio is the present-tense negated verb "I do not thank (you)." Se non here functions idiomatically as "except/only" followed by col core "with the heart" (i.e., only in my heart). A la paterna festa is a locative adverbial phrase "at the paternal (fatherly) feast/festival"—a poetic image for an inner, filial devotion.
segments.paradiso-15-g91: Ben is an adverb intensifier here meaning "indeed/well/earnestly." Supplico is the first-person present "I beseech/I beg." Io is the emphatic pronoun "I," and a te marks the indirect object "to you."
segments.paradiso-15-g92: Vivo topazio is a vocative phrase "living topaz" (addressing the jewel/personified gem). Che is the relative pronoun "who/that." Questa gioia prezïosa is the noun phrase "this precious joy." Ingemmi is a verb meaning "you (do) set with gems/adorn"; grammatically it is the verb of the relative clause describing the topaz (a present form used poetically to state that the topaz 'gemmes' this joy).
segments.paradiso-15-g93: Perché introduces a purposive/subjunctive clause here and means "so that/that." Mi is the indirect object "me." Facci is the (poetic/archaic) present subjunctive (2nd person) of fare meaning "you make" or "you grant." Del tuo nome sazio: del = di + il ("of the"/"by the"), tuo nome = "your name," and sazio is the predicate adjective "sated/satisfied." The whole clause asks that the addressee satisfy the speaker with (by) their name.
segments.paradiso-15-g94: O is the vocative exclamation "O/Oh." Fronda mia is a vocative "my frond/branch" (an image addressing a branch). In che introduces a relative clause "in which," io is emphatic "I," and compiacemmi is an archaic reflexive form meaning "I took pleasure/I pleased myself" (compiacere + mi). Pur is an adverb "even/still," and aspettando is a gerund "while waiting," completing the temporal image.
segments.paradiso-15-g95: Io fui is the passato remoto of essere "I was." La tua radice is the predicate "your root/origin." This is a simple declarative clause: "I was your root."
segments.paradiso-15-g96: Cotal is an archaic form of cotale ("such/a such"), and principio means "beginning/utterance/opening." Together they mean "such an opening/introduction."
segments.paradiso-15-g97: Rispondendo is the present gerund/participle of rispondere, "answering/responding," and it modifies principio to mean "such an opening, in answering/when replying."
segments.paradiso-15-g98: Femmi is an archaic contracted past-form with a clitic (likely from fare + mi, i.e. "fece mi" → "femmi") meaning roughly "was made to me/was given to me" or "it made me"; here it indicates that that opening/reply was produced/received by the speaker. The form is archaic and irregular compared with modern Italian.

sentences.paradiso-15-s5: Thus piously did the shade of Anchises stretch itself forth — if our greater Muse deserves to be believed — when in Elysium he became aware of his son.
sentences.paradiso-15-s6: "O my blood, O grace of God poured over (me/it), as to you, to whom twice the gate of heaven was ever opened?"
sentences.paradiso-15-s8: Thus that light: thereupon I turned to him; afterwards I turned my face to my lady, and I was amazed here and there; for within her eyes burned such a smile that I thought, with my own, to touch the depth of my glory and of my paradise.
sentences.paradiso-15-s9: Then, joyful to hear and to see, the spirit reached its own principle of things; I did not understand it, for it spoke so profoundly; nor did it hide itself from me by choice, but by necessity, because its concept was laid over the sign of mortal things.
sentences.paradiso-15-s10: And when the bow of ardent affection was so unstrung that the speech descended toward the sign of our intellect, the first thing that was perceived by me was, 'Blessed be you, threefold and one, who in my seed are so gracious!'
sentences.paradiso-15-s12: And he continued: 'Grateful and distant was the fast, drawn from reading the great volume where neither white nor dark ever changes; you have fulfilled it, my son, within this light in which I speak to you, by the grace of her who clothed your feathers for the high flight.'
sentences.paradiso-15-s25: She had no empty ancestral houses; Sardanapalus had not yet arrived there to show what can be done in a bedchamber.
sentences.paradiso-15-s26: Montemalo had not yet been conquered by your Uccellatoio, which, as it is overcome on the ascent, so will it be on the descent.
sentences.paradiso-15-s27: I saw Bellincion Berti going girded in leather and bone, and I saw his woman come from the mirror without a painted face; and I saw the one of the Nerli and the one of the Vecchio content with exposed skin, and his women at the spindle and at the distaff.
sentences.paradiso-15-s21: Then he said to me: "He of whom they say you are kin, and who for a hundred years and more has gone around the mountain in the first circle, was my son and was your great‑grandfather: it is fitting that you shorten for him the long toil by your works."
sentences.paradiso-15-s22: Florence, within the ancient circle, from which she still takes both the third and the ninth, stood at peace, sober and chaste.
sentences.paradiso-15-s23: She had no little chain, no crown, no ornate skirts, no belt that was meant to be seen more than the person.
sentences.paradiso-15-s24: When she was born, the daughter did not yet inspire fear in the father, for time and dowry did not, on this side and that, escape due measure.
sentences.paradiso-15-s13: You suppose that your thought comes to me from that which is First, just as, if it is known, the five and the six radiate from the One; and because I am, and because I may appear to you more joyful, do not ask me whether any other is in this joyous throng.
sentences.paradiso-15-s14: You believe correctly; for the lesser and the greater of this life look into the mirror in which, before you think, you lay open the thought; but because the sacred love in which I keep watch with perpetual sight and which satisfies me with sweet desire is better fulfilled, let your sure voice, bold and glad, sound to the will; let desire sound — to which my answer is already decreed!
sentences.paradiso-15-s16: I turned to Beatrice, and she heard it before I spoke, and she gave me a sign that made the wings of my will grow.
sentences.paradiso-15-s1: Benign will, in which the love that breathes straightforwardly is always liquefied; as avarice does in the unjust, silence was laid upon that sweet lyre, and caused the holy strings — which the right hand of heaven slackens and draws — to be stilled.
sentences.paradiso-15-s2: How deaf to the prayers of the righteous will those substances be — those beings which, in order to give me the desire that I might pray to them, were agreed to be silent?
sentences.paradiso-15-s3: It is right that he grieve without end who, for love of anything that does not last eternally, strips himself of that love.
sentences.paradiso-15-s4: Just as a sudden fire, hour after hour, runs through the serene, tranquil and pure heavens, moving the eyes that had been steady, and seems a star that changes place — except that nothing of it is lost from the side where it is kindled, and it itself endures little: — so, from the horn that stretches on the right at the foot of that cross ran a star of the constellation that shines there; nor did the gem part from its ribbon, but it ran along the radiating band, which appeared as fire behind alabaster.
sentences.paradiso-15-s33: Moronto was my brother and Eliseo; my lady came to me from the valley of the Po, and from that your surname came to be.
sentences.paradiso-15-s34: Then I followed the emperor Conrad; and he girded me with his soldiery, so that by doing good deeds I came into rank.
sentences.paradiso-15-s35: After them I went to confront the wickedness of that law, whose people usurp — through the fault of the shepherds — your justice.
sentences.paradiso-15-s36: There I was, by that shameful people freed from the deceitful world, whose love defiles many souls; and I came from martyrdom to this peace.
sentences.paradiso-15-s29: Each one was sure of its grave, and as yet no part of France lay deserted in the bed.
sentences.paradiso-15-s30: One kept watch over the cradle with devoted care, and, comforting it, used the idiom that formerly the fathers and mothers used to amuse; the other, drawing her hair toward the fortress, told tales with her household of the Trojans, of Fiesole and of Rome.
sentences.paradiso-15-s31: At such a marvel then a Cianghella or a Lapo Salterello would be considered remarkable, which now would be Cincinnatus and Corniglia.
sentences.paradiso-15-s32: Given such repose, such a fine civic life, such faithful citizenship, such a sweet lodging, Mary gave me, called out in loud cries; and in your ancient Baptistery I was at once a Christian and Cacciaguida.
sentences.paradiso-15-s17: Then I began thus: "Affection and reason, as the first equality appeared to you, were made into one weight for each of you, because the sun that lit you and burned you is so equal in heat and in light that all comparisons are inadequate."
sentences.paradiso-15-s18: But will and reason among mortals, for the cause that is manifest to you, are differently feathered in their wings; therefore I, who am mortal, feel myself in this inequality, and so I give thanks only with my heart at the paternal feast.
sentences.paradiso-15-s19: I earnestly beg you, living topaz who jewels this precious joy, that you satisfy me with your name.
sentences.paradiso-15-s20: "O my frond in which I took my pleasure even while waiting, I was your root": such an opening, answering, was made to me.
